coredata.dc:title: Dual-emission carbon dots-based biosensor for polarity/targeting bimodal recognition and mild photothermal therapy of tumor
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Dong, Xiaorui
Yan, Wenjun
Zhang, Dongmei
Wang, Ruihan
Xue, Liuyan
Shi, Heping
Li, Yingqi
coredata.dc:description: It is essential to develop a multifunctional nanoplatform for biosensing, tumor diagnosis and treatment simultaneously. Herein, dual-emission fluorescent carbon dots (HA-CDs) were fabricated via a one-pot solvothermal method using spinach powder as carbon source and hyaluronic acid (HA) as targeting agent. The obtained HA-CDs exhibited outstanding optical properties, good targeted tumor and excellent photothermal conversion performance. Interestingly, HA-CDs can sensitively perceive the changes in polar environments attributed to the inherent ratiometric fluorescence characteristics, and combined with the intrinsic targeting tumor ability achieved tumor cell recognition. More importantly, the HA-CDs possess good photothermal conversion efficiency of 21.2 % to be beneficial for photothermal therapy of tumors. The survival rate of HeLa cells incubated with HA-CDs dramatically decreased to 14 % after 660 nm laser irradiation, revealing the significant tumor inhibition of HA-CDs in vitro. Notably, through individual intraperitoneal and intratumoral injection, it was found that HA-CDs demonstrated a similar tumor suppressed effect on 4T1 tumor-bearing mice exposed to laser irradiation, fully uncovering that HA-CDs can efficiently accumulate at tumor site by intraperitoneal injection. Besides, the histopathological analysis of major organs ex vivo revealed a good biosafety profile. Collectively, this strategy of designed HA-CDs provides a new multifunctional nanoplatform for potential clinical application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Dual-emission fluorescent
Polarity
Tumor-target
Tumor cells recognition
Photothermal therapy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Polarity and targeted dual pattern recognition of tumor cells
PTT performance of HA-CDs in vitro
PTT performance of HA-CDs in vivo

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image (Inset: Size distribution), (B) FTIR spectrum, (C–F) XPS full spectrum, C1s, N1s and O1s spectra, (G) Zeta potential, and (H) UV–vis absorption, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra (Insets: photographs of HA-CDs solution under daylight (left) and UV light (right), respectively.) of HA-CDs. (I) Fluorescence emission spectra of HA-CDs under different excitation.

(A) Confocal fluorescence images of HeLa cells stained with HA-CDs and stimulated with DMSO and sucrose, respectively. (B) Intracellular mean fluorescence intensities of HeLa cells in Fig. 2(A). (C) Intracellular fluorescence intensity ratio of red channel for HA-CDs + DMSO or HA-CDs + Sucrose and HA-CDs treated cells.

(A) Confocal fluorescence images of HeLa cells. From top to bottom: blank HeLa cell group, the HA-CDs-incubated HeLa cells for 2 h without or with 8.0 mg mL−1 HA pretreated cells, respectively. (B) Intracellular mean fluorescence intensities of three groups in Fig. 3A. (C) Intracellular fluorescence intensity ratio of red channel for HA-CDs and HA + HA-CDs treated cells. (D–E) Uptake of HA-CDs to HeLa cells by flow cytometry. (F) The quantitative mean fluorescence intensity of Fig. 3D and E.

(A) Confocal fluorescence images of HL-7702 cells and HeLa cells incubated with HA-CDs. (B) Intracellular mean fluorescence intensities of HL-7702 cells and HeLa cells from Fig. 4(A). (C) Intracellular fluorescence intensity ratio of red channel for HeLa cells and HL-7702 cells. (D–E) Uptake of HA-CDs to HeLa cells and HL-7702 cells by flow cytometry. (F) The quantitative mean fluorescence intensity of Fig. 4D and E.

(A) Temperature changes of HA-CDs solution at different laser power densities upon 660 nm irradiation. (B) Temperature change curves of HA-CDs aqueous solutions with various concentrations under 660 nm laser irradiation (0.6 W cm−2) for 6 min. (C) Photothermal stability of HA-CDs over five cycles of laser on/off (660 nm, 0.6 W cm−2). (D) Cell viability of HA-CDs on HeLa cells with (660 nm, 0.6 W cm−2, 5 min) or without laser irradiation by the MTT assay. (E) Live-dead staining images of HeLa cells with Calcein-AM (green, live cells) and PI (red, dead cells) after different treatments.

(A) The average tumor volume variation in mice after photothermal treatment (660 nm, 0.6 W cm−2, 15 min) at different time. (B) The tumor weight of excised tumors in saline and HA-CDs groups. (Inset: Representative photos of excised tumors in saline and HA-CDs groups.) (C) Average body weight of mice treated with saline and HA-CDs during photothermal treatment. (D) Organ index of 4T1 tumor-bearing mice treated with saline and HA-CDs, in which mice without tumors served as the control group (n = 3). (E) Histopathological analysis by H&E staining of dissected tissues (heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney and tumor) after treatment with saline and HA-CDs, respectively. (Mice in control group are healthy without tumor inoculation, ∗p < 0.05, Scale bar: 200 μm).
Schematic illustration for preparation of HA-CDs and the applications in bimodal polarity/targeting recognition and mild PTT in vitro and in vivo for tumor treatment.
Schematic diagram of the preparation process of HA-CDs.

head.ce:title: Dual-emission carbon dots-based biosensor for polarity/targeting bimodal recognition and mild photothermal therapy of tumor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, 030006, P. R. China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analytical Instrumentation Center, Institute of Coal Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Taiyuan, 030001, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Taiyuan Central Hospital, Taiyuan, 030009, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Taiyuan Central Hospital, Taiyuan 030009, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is essential to develop a multifunctional nanoplatform for biosensing, tumor diagnosis and treatment simultaneously. Herein, dual-emission fluorescent carbon dots (HA-CDs) were fabricated via a one-pot solvothermal method using spinach powder as carbon source and hyaluronic acid (HA) as targeting agent. The obtained HA-CDs exhibited outstanding optical properties, good targeted tumor and excellent photothermal conversion performance. Interestingly, HA-CDs can sensitively perceive the changes in polar environments attributed to the inherent ratiometric fluorescence characteristics, and combined with the intrinsic targeting tumor ability achieved tumor cell recognition. More importantly, the HA-CDs possess good photothermal conversion efficiency of 21.2 % to be beneficial for photothermal therapy of tumors. The survival rate of HeLa cells incubated with HA-CDs dramatically decreased to 14 % after 660 nm laser irradiation, revealing the significant tumor inhibition of HA-CDs in vitro. Notably, through individual intraperitoneal and intratumoral injection, it was found that HA-CDs demonstrated a similar tumor suppressed effect on 4T1 tumor-bearing mice exposed to laser irradiation, fully uncovering that HA-CDs can efficiently accumulate at tumor site by intraperitoneal injection. Besides, the histopathological analysis of major organs ex vivo revealed a good biosafety profile. Collectively, this strategy of designed HA-CDs provides a new multifunctional nanoplatform for potential clinical application.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer, a highly invasive disease with high mortality, is caused by abnormal cell division, heterogeneous cell growth, and ultimately to form a malignant neoplasm, which seriously threatens human health [1,2]. To solve this thorny problem, more and more innovative approaches have been widely utilized to diagnosis and treatment of cancer. For example, various nanomaterials were developed as drug carriers to deliver drugs such as doxorubicin, photosensitizers for chemotherapy and photodynamic therapy [3,4]. In addition, many imaging techniques were adopted for tumor diagnosis including X-ray computed tomography, magnetic resonance imaging, and ultrasound [5,6]. These nanotechnologies have made great progress for tumor treatment, but they still remain some drawbacks, involving high cost, poor specificity, excessive radiation, low signal-to-noise ratio, and single function [7]. Among them, fluorescence imaging technology, as a powerful visual detection tool for biological systems, has received increasing attention due to its superiority of non-invasive radiation, high spatiotemporal resolution and low cost [8–10].

Polarity, as an indispensable microenvironment parameter in biological systems, acts an important role in many physiology processes involving inactivating functional proteins and triggering the signal transduction [11,12]. Abnormal polarity change causes the occurrence and development of pathophysiological inflammation and cancer [13]. Some reports have proved that cancer cells exhibited lower polarity compared to normal cells [14,15]. By utilizing this difference, precise recognition of cancer cells can be achieved. For example, Wang et al. [16] developed a lipid droplets-targeted near-infrared (NIR) carbon dots (CDs) with polarity-ultrasensitive property to achieve liver cancer diagnosis. Shi et al. [17] prepared a red emissive CDs to successfully discriminate cancer cells based on polarity differences. However, most of these probes rely on a single fluorescence emission, which often suffers from photobleaching, low sensitivity, complex synthesis, and interference from uneven distribution [18,19]. Fortunately, ratiometric fluorescent probes can enhanced self-calibration ability to improve detection accuracy by intensity ratio of two well-resolved emission peaks [20,21]. These features make ratiometric fluorescent probes have important applications in environmental monitoring, biochemical sensing, life sciences, immune analysis, etc [3].

Photothermal therapy (PTT), that utilizes local heat generated by photothermal agents (PTAs) under light irradiation to effectuate tumor ablation, is considered as a useful approach for cancer treatment due to its unique superiority of non-invasive, oxygen-independent, and precise targeting [22]. It has been reported that irreversible tissue damage will occur when temperature reaches 42 °C. Whereas heating of tissue above 49 °C results in cell necrosis [23]. Currently, various PTAs were designed for PTT, including precious metal materials [24], organic small molecules [25], two-dimensional materials [26], semiconducting polymer nanoparticles [27] and carbon-based nanomaterials [28]. Among them, CDs possess a series of prominent characteristics, such as superior solubility, small size, tunable fluorescence, easy surface modification, good photostability, and excellent biocompatibility [29,30], which endow them enormous potential in biological and biomedical applications. Moreover, some CDs with red or NIR absorption demonstrate effective photothermal conversion for PTT in a non-invasive manner [31–33]. For instance, Zhao et al. [31] designed the CDs with PCE of 54.7 % as efficient NIR light-triggered PTT against cancer. Phuong et al. [32] investigated a visible-light and pH-responsive TiO2-integrated cross-linked CDs for tumor detection and controllable PTT with PCE of 9.54 %. Li et al. [33] fabricated NIR-II emission CDs to fight against tumors by PTT. Unfortunately, most of reported CDs have several drawbacks: (1) Most CDs exhibit poor organizational penetration ability owing to their short emission wavelength, low fluorescence quantum yield (QY) and insufficient fluorescence intensity, which greatly limits their application in biomedical fields [34]; (2) The majority of CDs as a sensor only hold single emission characteristic and it is susceptible to be disturbed by external factors [35]; (3) Due to insufficient specific tumor binding capacity, many CDs-based nano-drug cannot reach into tumor tissues effectively to result in some side effects [36]. In addition, most of current CDs with tumor target ability are prepared through cumbersome processes [37]; (4) Another drawback of CDs is single-function, only for sensing [38] or therapy [33]. Thus, it is imperative to design CDs with multifunctionality including effective targeting ability, simple preparation process, excellent emission, good biocompatibility, and desirable PTT.

Hyaluronic acid (HA), a natural polysaccharide with non-toxicity, good biocompatibility, biodegradability, non-immunogenicity and receptor binding properties [37,39,40], has become an appealing target reagent to bind with CD44 receptors overexpressed on the surface of various tumor cells [41], thereby considering as one of the optimal candidate materials for drug delivery to accumulate in tumor site. In addition, biomass, owing to its advantages of safety, low cost, easy availability, environmental friendliness, and easy self-doping, is becoming fascinating precursor for CDs-acquired. In nature, chlorophyll in green plants possess strong NIR absorption and emission due to porphyrin structure. Unfortunately, the hydrophobic as well as unstable porphyrin cannot be used as a fluorescent sensor for bioimaging directly [42]. Moreover, CDs-prepared from green plants as precursors lack targeted tumor properties so as to poor application in tumor therapy.

Herein, dual-emission CDs were designed using a facile one-step approach, which would not only be fit for selective tumor recognition by utilizing the low polarity and surface overexpression of CD44 receptors in cancer cells, but also CDs exhibited significant targeted PTT with noticeable antitumor activity. As Scheme 1 indicated, dual-emission fluorescent CDs (HA-CDs) at 496 nm and 680 nm were achieved through spinach powder containing rich chlorophyll level as carbon source and HA as a targeting agent. Intriguingly, the HA-CDs demonstrated satisfactory polarity-sensitivity and excellent tumor-target ability, which contributes to its outstanding in vivo tumor-targeting capacity. More importantly, the obvious absorption at 665 nm of HA-CDs with good PCE as high as 21.2 % endowed them efficient PTT effect under 660 nm laser irradiation with a laser power density of 0.6 W cm−2 to achieve tumor suppression.
The detailed experimental steps were mentioned in supplementary material.
As depicted in Scheme 2 , dual-emission fluorescent CDs (HA-CDs) were fabricated through a one-pot solvothermal method using spinach powder and HA as precursors in anhydrous ethanol.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image (Fig. 1 A) displayed that average diameter of monodispersed HA-CDs is 1.99 ± 0.26 nm (n = 100). While the hydrodynamic diameter of HA-CDs measured was 78.9 nm by the dynamic light scatting (DLS) (Fig. S1A), which is much larger than that of measured by TEM, speculating attributed to the interaction between surface functional groups of HA-CDs through hydrogen bonds, van der Waals forces, secondary bonds, etc. Besides, the stability of HA-CDs was measured via DLS within 72 h. It can be seen from Fig. S1B that there was no significant change in the size of HA-CDs with the storage time, indicating good stability of HA-CDs. The fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectrum (Fig. 1B) indicated the presence of stretching vibrations of O–H/N–H (3407 cm−1), C–H (2982 cm−1 and 2930 cm−1), CO (1633 cm−1), CN (1465 cm−1), C–O (1398 cm−1), C–N (1054 cm−1) and bending vibrations of N–H (1633 cm−1), =C–H (785 cm−1), O–H (609 cm−1) in HA-CDs. Compared to CDs uncoated HA (Fig. S2A), the HA-CDs did not emerge any new functional groups, only slight increase in transmittance attributed to more –OH and –COOH on the surface of HA-CDs after introduction of HA, which clarifies that HA-CDs has weaker absorption ability for infrared light compared to the CDs uncoated HA and the formation of HA-CDs. Meanwhile, the full X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum (Fig. 1C) of HA-CDs showed three typical peaks at 281.9 eV (C1s), 397.5 eV (N1s) and 528.9 eV (O1s), suggesting that HA-CDs consists of C, N and O elements. From Fig. 1D, it can be found that C1s spectrum contains two noticeable peaks at 284.8 eV for CC/C–C and 285.9 eV for C–O/C–N. N1s spectrum (Fig. 1E) displays two peaks at 400.1 eV and 402.6 eV, indicating the existence of C–N and N–H. In O1s spectrum (Fig. 1F), there are two distinct peaks for CO (532.1 eV) and C–O–C/C–OH (532.9 eV). Moreover, the zeta potential (Fig. 1G) of HA-CDs (−15.6 mV) was lower than that of CDs (−10.6 mV) (Fig. S2B), revealing that the abundant –OH and –COOH were exposed to the surface of HA-CDs in the presence of HA, which proves the formation of HA-CDs. These results prove the presence of hydrophilic functional groups of −OH, and –NH2 and abundant –COOH on the surface of HA-CDs, supporting the presence of HA and revealing that HA-CDs is suitable for target tumor.

The HA-CDs exhibited four absorption peaks at 205, 279, 416, and 665 nm in water medium (Fig. 1H). The peaks located at 205 and 279 nm are characteristic of π–π∗ transition of carbon core as commonly observed in CDs [43]. The peaks at 416 and 665 nm may be derived from n–π∗ transition of CO and CN bonds within porphyrin structures [44]. By comparing the optical properties of CDs and HA-CDs, it was found that the UV–vis absorption peak of HA-CDs was red shifted from 659 nm to 665 nm (Fig. S2C), and the emission of HA-CDs at 680 nm was slightly weaker than that of CDs uncoated HA (Fig. S2D), indicating that the existence of HA in HA-CDs. Moreover, two emission peaks at 496 and 680 nm after excitation at 410 nm are also displayed in Fig. 1H, which may be attributed to carbon core state and surface state [45], respectively. The photographs of HA-CDs solution presented in Fig. 1H inset show the light brown under daylight (left) and red fluorescence under UV light (right), respectively. Besides, as exhibited in Fig. 1I, the HA-CDs manifest double separated fluorescence emission bands in blue-to-yellow (400 − 600 nm) and red to NIR (600 − 800 nm) emission regions under different excitation. The emission in blue-to-yellow range displays an excitation dependent feature, whereas the red–NIR emission reveals an excitation independent characteristic with the maximum emission peak at 680 nm. This interesting phenomenon provides good information for HA-CDs as ratiometric fluorescent probes for eliminating background interference and improving detection accuracy. Of note, the HA-CDs has a high fluorescence QY of 25.4 % by selecting Rhodamine B (QY = 89 % in ethanol) as the reference (Fig. S3, Tables S1 and S2), which is higher than most reported results [42,46], and a comparison was listed in Table S3. Additionally, the experimental results confirmed that HA-CDs have high fluorescence stability (Fig. S4) and specific response to Cu2+ (Fig. S5A) with the linear range of 200 μM–800 μM (R2 = 0.993) (Figs. S5B–C), as well as the limit of detection (LOD) of 0.247 μM, which is better than or comparable to the reported CDs-based Cu2+ sensors (Table S4). To elucidate the fluorescence quenching mechanism, firstly, whether a complex is formed between HA-CDs and Cu2+ was being investigated. It was found that the zeta potential of HA-CDs solution changed from −15.6 mV to 0.42 mV after the addition of Cu2+ in Fig. S6A, confirming the successful coordination of Cu2+ with HA-CDs. Additionally, the UV–vis absorption peaks for HA-CDs were slightly decreased and blue-shift after introducing Cu2+ (Fig. S6B), and different from the absorption peaks of Cu2+, which supported that the formation of stable complexes between HA-CDs and Cu2+. These are tentatively speculated to be static quenching mechanisms caused by the formation of ground state complexes [24]. Secondly, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of Cu2+ was compared with the excitation and emission spectra of HA-CDs. As displayed in Fig. S6C, there is almost no overlap between the UV–vis absorption spectrum of Cu2+ and the fluorescence emission spectrum of HA-CDs, so the quenching caused by inner filter effect can be excluded. Then, based on the slope of the straight line (I496/I680)－[Cu2+] as a function of temperature, the mechanism of fluorescence quenching was further investigated. If the temperature increases and the slope increases, it indicates dynamic quenching; If the temperature increases and the slope decreases, then static quenching occurs [47]. It was clearly visible in Fig. S6D that the slope of the straight line for (I496/I680)－[Cu2+] decreases as the temperature increases, confirming the static quenching mechanism of HA-CDs fluorescence quenching caused by Cu2+.

The fluorescence spectra of HA-CDs were tested in a variety of solvents to explore the response of HA-CDs toward polarity circumstance. The fluorescence intensity prominently enhanced with the decrease of solvent polarity (Fig. S7), proving that HA-CDs possess polarity responsiveness. Additionally, the fluorescence spectra of HA-CDs were recorded in ethanol/H2O mixed system (volume ratio) to prove the polarity-sensitivity of HA-CDs. As the polarity increases, the red fluorescence intensity dramatically decreased, on the contrary the blue emission is almost unchanged (Fig. S8A). According to the relationship between I496/I680 and ethanol content, an exponential function curve was firstly fitted (Fig. S8B), and the curve equation is I496/I680 = 0.37 + 101.41 e−0.09[ethanol content] (R2 = 0.9858). In order to facilitate practical application, the exponential function was converted into a linear relationship with a linear equation of ln (I496/I680 − 0.37) = −0.098 [ethanol content] + 5.15 (R2 = 0.9916) (Fig. S8C). The similar linear equations containing a subtraction item have also appeared in reported references [48,49]. The quenching of red fluorescence with the increase of water content may be attributed to the protonation of nitrogen atoms and the hydrolysis of carboxyl groups on CDs, where the increasing number of protons transfer obviously decreases the red fluorescence as the water ion concentration increases [50]. Inspired by this phenomenon, we further use HA-CDs for the detection of ethanol in actual samples such as hand disinfectant solution. The hand disinfectant solution with a standard ethanol content of 80 % ± 5 % (v/v) was diluted with water to obtain the final ethanol content of 40 %, 60 %, and 80 %, and then added to HA-CDs system. The determined contents of ethanol were 42.2 %, 57.4 %, and 77.6 %, respectively. The recoveries were found to be 95.7 − 105.5 % with relative standard deviation (RSD) of 0.27 – 0.47 % (Table S5). Therefore, HA-CDs provide a promising tool for the detection of ethanol content in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Polarity and targeted dual pattern recognition of tumor cells
ce:section.ce:para: The above results demonstrated that HA-CDs has polarity sensing characteristics, so HA-CDs is speculated to be potential as a probe for polarity imaging in complicated biological environments. Before biological applications, the cytotoxicity of HA-CDs on HL-7702 (normal cells) and HeLa (tumor cells) cells were first performed via MTT assay. The cell viability (Fig. S9A) exceeded 80 % as the concentration of HA-CDs reached 1.0 mg mL−1, revealing that HA-CDs possess excellent biocompatibility. Interestingly, as the concentration of HA-CDs increases, HA-CDs is only toxic to tumor cells and is basically non-toxic to normal cells, indicating that high concentrations of HA-CDs have selective tumor toxicity. It is speculated that cancer cells death may be caused by an increase in ROS level and this conjecture was supported by detecting ROS signals in tumor cells through cells were treated using high concentration HA-CDs (Fig. S9B), but not in normal cells (Fig. S9B). To deeply explain this phenomenon, the glutathione (GSH) consumption capability by HA-CDs was verified using the 5, 5′-dithiobis (2-nitrobenzoic acid) (DTNB) as a probe. Pure DTNB exhibited a characteristic absorption peak at 325 nm (The black line in Fig. S9C) and a new absorption peak emerged around 412 nm following the addition of GSH (The red line in Fig. S9C). It is evident that the absorbance at 412 nm decreases when HA-CDs was present in DTNB + GSH medium (The green line in Fig. S9C), indicating that the consumption of GSH to disrupt the redox equilibrium in tumor cells, which triggered an increase of ROS level to cause the tumor cell death.

Therefore, the ability of HA-CDs for monitoring the polarity variation of living cells was explored within the safe HA-CDs concentration. It is reported that cancer cells exhibited lower polarity compared to normal cells [13,14] and DMSO can be quickly absorbed by living cells to decrease the intracellular polarity, as well as the sucrose with high concentration will increase polarity of living cells [51]. It is observed that HeLa cells treated by DMSO show approximately twice enhanced fluorescence in red channel, while slightly changed in blue channel compared with cells-treated by HA-CDs alone as a control group (Fig. 2 A–C). By contrast, the red fluorescence of HeLa cells-treated by sucrose is about 0.5 times lower than that of control group and the blue fluorescence remained constant (Fig. 2A–C), proving that HA-CDs can be used to sense the change of polarity in living cells. As a control, the non-targeted CDs also exhibit polarity sensitivity (Fig. S10), but the fluorescence intensity is lower than that of HA-CDs, revealing the targeting ability of HA-CDs, as confirmed by Fig. 3 .

As seen in Fig. 3A, the red fluorescence intensity of HeLa cells-pretreated with HA is obviously lower than that of HeLa cells-treated with HA-CDs alone, and the quantitative data showed that the latter was 3 times higher than the former (Fig. 3B and C). Additionally, the same conclusion was obtained by the flow cytometry (Fig. 3D–F) once again, which suggested that HA-CDs have good tumor targeting ability.

Taking above advantages, the HA-CDs were utilized to distinguish cancer cells from normal cells by confocal fluorescence imaging. As shown in Fig. 4 A-C, the HeLa cells incubated with HA-CDs for 20 min emit stronger red fluorescence than the HL-7702 cells (about 5-fold). The same conclusion was also obtained from flow cytometry (Fig. 4D–F). As a control, the CDs without HA also achieved cancer cells recognition based on polarity differences (Fig. S11), but this difference of IHeLa/HL-7702 is smaller than that of HA-CDs, unfolding the targeted property of HA-CDs. Therefore, the HA-CDs can successfully distinguish cancer cells from normal cells based on polarity differences and significant targeting ability without any other chemical treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PTT performance of HA-CDs in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: HA-CDs solutions showed excellent absorption in 650–700 nm region, implying that HA-CDs has red laser-triggered photothermal conversion capacity. The temperature variation of HA-CDs solution exhibited obvious power density-dependent (Fig. 5 A) and concentration-dependent behavior (Fig. 5B). And the highest temperature increased by 27.9 °C after 6 min irradiation (660 nm, 0.6 W cm−2) at the HA-CDs concentration of 2.00 mg mL−1, which is sufficient to destroy tumor, while the temperature of pure water only increased by 5.1 °C, uncovering that HA-CDs can efficiently absorb red light and convert photo energy into thermal energy to achieve tumor ablation. As noted in Fig. 5C, after five cycles of laser on/off, no significant temperature changes were measured throughout entire process, proving the great photothermal stability of HA-CDs and the corresponding PCE was determined to be 21.2 %, which is comparable to the reported results and sufficient to achieve PTT effects [24,32,52,53] (Table S3). These results implied the good light-to-heat conversion ability of HA-CDs as good PTT process.

To further assess the photothermal effect of the HA-CDs in vitro, HeLa cells were firstly employed to determine PTT effect. As depicted in Fig. 5D, the HeLa cell viability was above 80 % within the HA-CDs concentration range of 0 – 1.0 mg mL−1 without laser irradiation, showing that HA-CDs have negligible toxicity to HeLa cells in concentration range of 0 – 1.0 mg mL−1 without laser irradiation. On the contrary, the cell viability was dramatically decreased with the increasing concentration of HA-CDs under 660 nm irradiation (0.6 W cm−2) for 5 min, and the cellular viability was observed to be only 14 % when the concentration of HA-CDs is 1.0 mg mL−1, indicating high PTT of HA-CDs. Furthermore, Calcein-AM and PI were performed to stain living cells (green fluorescence) and dead cells (red fluorescence) to visually verify the PTT effects-mediated by HA-CDs on HeLa cells. As seen in Fig. 5E, the whole regions of HeLa cells demonstrated bright green fluorescence in the control only with laser irradiation or HA-CDs (1.0 mg mL−1) treatment, declaring that most HeLa cells have not been damaged. Conversely, the HeLa cells treated with HA-CDs (0.5, 1.0 mg mL−1) exhibited strong red fluorescence when continuously exposed to a 660 nm laser (0.6 W cm−2, 5 min), revealing effective cancer cell destruction by HA-CDs plus laser irradiation. These findings validated the satisfactory PTT efficacy of HA-CDs under laser irradiation (660 nm, 0.6 W cm−2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: PTT performance of HA-CDs in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: Inspired by the excellent PTT therapeutic effect of HA-CDs in vitro, the PTT efficacy of HA-CDs for 4T1 tumor-bearing mice were further assessed by HA-CDs administrated through intraperitoneal and intratumoral injections. As seen in Fig. 6 A and Fig. S12, the tumors-treated by HA-CDs were markedly suppressed after 8 days by laser irradiation, and the tumor volume was almost one-third of the salt-treated mice, revealing HA-CDs has excellent PTT therapeutic effect for tumors. In addition, the tumors were excised from the mice after euthanasia to record the tumor weights and representative photographs of tumors were also displayed in Fig. 6B. Clearly, the tumor of saline group did not inhibit tumor growth, nevertheless the HA-CDs + laser irradiation group showed a clear reduction in tumor weight and volume, indicating effective PTT with HA-CDs. Furthermore, no markedly unusual fluctuation in the body weight of mice (Fig. 6C), no abnormal clinical symptoms or behaviors, such as anorexia, malaise, or death were observed during the treatment period and no obvious differences were found in heart, liver, lung and kidney indices and pathological analysis among control groups without transplanted tumors, saline group and HA-CDs groups (Fig. 6D and E), indicating that HA-CDs do not cause prominent toxicity and side effects. However, the spleens indice of saline group become markedly enlarged (Fig. 6D) due to massive extramedullary hematopoiesis [54], which was as well confirmed by pathological analysis (Fig. 6E). Besides, the saline group mice had acute and chronic inflammation in the lung, which probable since the excessive volume of tumors destroying the immune system.

The HA-CDs-treated mice showed a small amount of neutrophil and plasma cell infiltration in the portal vein portal area of the liver, and visible extramedullary hematopoiesis in the spleen, but weaker than that of saline group. The other major organ slices are normal and do not exhibit obvious pathological changes, testifying that HA-CDs hold low toxicity to the main organs of mice. In addition, tumor slices of all groups show significant necrosis, and the necrosis of tumor slices in the HA-CDs group were severer than that in the saline group, implying that HA-CDs can efficiently accumulate at tumor sites to achieve tumor suppression. Notably, through intraperitoneal injection and intratumoral injection, it was found that HA-CDs has a similar inhibitory effect on 4T1 tumor-bearing mice exposed to laser irradiation, fully indicating that HA-CDs has significant tumor targeting ability by intraperitoneal injection. All above results demonstrated that HA-CDs emerge satisfactory PTT effect in vivo.

Summarily, a simple, green, and economic one-step method for preparing HA-CDs have been developed by using spinach powder as carbon source and HA as targeting ligand for CD44 receptors in cancer cells. The as-prepared HA-CDs exhibited excellent photostability, good biocompatibility, and cell penetration capacity. Taking advantages of good polarity-sensitivity and tumor-target ability, the HA-CDs have been successfully used to identification of cancer cells. Furthermore, the HA-CDs possess good photothermal properties with PCE of 21.2 %. Both in vitro and in vivo experimental results demonstrated the high anticancer efficacy after PTT treatment under 660 nm laser irradiation with a power laser density of 0.6 W cm−2. This work provides a new strategy for the design of multifunctional CDs-based nanoplatform that integrates sensing, tumor diagnosis, and treatment, thereby opening a new horizon for exploring multifunctional CDs.

Xiaorui Dong: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Wenjun Yan: Software, Investigation. Dongmei Zhang: Validation, Software. Ruihan Wang: Software. Liuyan Xue: Software. Heping Shi: Validation, Investigation. Yingqi Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
All animal experiments were performed according to protocols approved by the Radiation Protection Institute of Drug Safety Evaluation Center in China (Production license: SYXK (Jin) 2023-0007).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the Central Government Guiding Local Science and Technology Development (Grant No. YDZX20191400002477), the Shanxi Province Natural Science Foundation (Grant No. 202203021221003), Shanxi Province Patent Transformation Project (Grant No. 202304015), CIRP Open Fund of Radiation Protection Laboratories (Grant No. CIRP-CAEA20220203), Interdisciplinary Construction Project of Shanxi University (Grant No. 113541028).
